The witness cell arrived with six roles and no confidence.

Local authority: Sefa and Lio jointly, with Halren holding personal stop authority.

Care lead: Pella, because the first deployment had been too important to delegate cleanly.

Memory witness: Iris, though she argued that a local song keeper should take the role and lost only because the family had not invited one.

Technical listener: Lian, remote through a low-band relay and forbidden to request instrument use unless asked.

Dissent holder: Talen Ash, the hostile Congregation witness, whose presence made everyone uncomfortable and therefore useful.

Cost lead: Mara, remote, because wage loss, travel, and grief time needed counting before anyone pretended the visit was free.

Transport and custody support waited outside with Fay and the Unwoven carrier who had leaked preservation tips. Corin remained at the tax office, which annoyed him less than it would have a month earlier and therefore annoyed him in a new way.

The first hour was tea.

That was Pella's decision. No forms before tea unless immediate harm demanded it. Halren made the tea too strong. Sefa corrected the steeping time. Lio corrected the water. Both corrections came with the practiced irritation of daughters who had not lost the right to be annoyed by a living father.

Iris wrote nothing until Halren said, "Write that. They both think I make bad tea in both worlds."

The room laughed.

Small laughter had become one of the coalition's most valuable diagnostic tools. It did not prove safety. It showed where bodies could still move.

After tea, Pella reviewed stop authority. Halren could leave. Either daughter could stop. The cell could stop if distress rose too fast. No one would ask which funeral was real. No one would ask Halren to perform gratitude for being alive. No one would use the session for compensation, doctrine, or public proof without later consent.

Talen Ash asked to add one clause.

Everyone tensed.

"No one will call the daughters faithless for remembering his death."

Vessa, listening remotely, said nothing. That silence felt like approval with its teeth kept in.

The clause stayed.

Sefa spoke first. Rain funeral. Mud on the hems. A borrowed cart stuck outside the wash. Lio singing because their mother had lost her voice. Halren's hands wrapped in white cloth. A cup placed upside down on the threshold afterward. She described it with enough detail that the room began to smell rain that was not there.

Lio shook throughout but did not interrupt.

When Sefa finished, Pella called a body check. Sefa hated it and answered anyway. Throat tight, hands cold, no current-time loss, wanted to continue. Halren had moved to the doorway but had not left.

Lio spoke next. Heat funeral. The ground too hard. No cart because the lower road was clear. Sefa silent, not singing. Their mother alive in this version, placing salt on Halren's tongue before burial because old custom said workers should not enter earth thirsty. White cloth again. Upside-down cup again.

The cup became the first shared object.

Iris asked permission to focus on it.

Both daughters agreed. Halren did too after asking whether cups could become dangerous. Pella answered, "Anything can if people use it badly."

"Good," he said. "Honest."

The cup still existed. It sat on a shelf near the stove, chipped at the rim. In the current household story, it had belonged to Halren's mother and was used during sickness season to measure bitter tonic. In both funeral memories, it had been turned upside down on the threshold.

Lian's remote voice came through softly. "The cup can serve as material witness if the family consents. No field yet. Just placement."

Sefa placed the cup on the table.

Lio turned it upside down.

Halren inhaled sharply.

"Stop?" Pella asked.

He shook his head. "No. It is just ugly seeing your own absence know where the shelf is."

Iris wrote that down after asking.

The cell did not activate D-17. Not yet. They built the memory table by hand first. Rain. Heat. White cloth. Cup. Singing. Silence. Mother voiceless. Mother alive and speaking salt words. Road blocked. Road clear. Halren present in current time. Halren absent in both daughter memories. Shared year events. No known intervention. Increasing drift after regional tremors in the Resonance instruments.

Talen Ash's role as dissent holder became unexpectedly important when everyone began leaning toward the cup as anchor. He raised one hand.

"You are all making the cup carry agreement because you are relieved it appears in both memories."

Pella looked annoyed and then thoughtful.

Iris nodded slowly. "Correct."

Lian added, "Shared object does not mean shared meaning."

Sefa said, "In mine, the cup means the house closed."

Lio said, "In mine, it means he was sent with water."

Halren said, "In mine, it means fever medicine."

Three meanings. One cup.

The protocol, when they finally used it, did not try to choose.

The anchor stones were not brought inside until Halren asked what would happen if the memories kept getting louder. Pella explained the risk. Lian explained the limits. Iris explained that using the protocol would not make either funeral official. Talen explained, awkwardly but earnestly, that he distrusted the tool and would say if he thought it began deciding for them. That helped Halren more than the technical explanation.

"Good," Halren said. "Bring in someone who does not love it."

The stones entered in a plain cloth bag.

No one called them sacred.

They placed three anchors:

Personal witness: Sefa's rain account.

Personal witness in dissent: Lio's heat account.

Material witness: the cup, with all three meanings attached.

Current-time witness: Halren's living presence, seated by the door, free to leave.

Lian hesitated. "That is four anchors."

"Is that unsafe?" Pella asked.

"It is less stable."

Halren said, "So are we."

No one improved that.

The field began unevenly. Four tones, not three, and one of them kept fading: Halren's current-time witness. Not because he was weak. Because the protocol had been built around contested memory, not living contradiction. Lian almost adjusted the field, then stopped.

"I need consent to strengthen current-time anchor," she said.

Halren asked, "What does that mean without polite words?"

"The tool is paying more attention to the funerals than to you breathing."

"Then make it less stupid."

Consent recorded.

Lian adjusted.

The fourth tone steadied.

Sefa began crying. "I don't want to lose the rain."

Lio said, "I don't want him dead in heat forever."

Halren said, "I don't want to become proof that neither of you mourned."

The tones did not harmonize. They moved around each other, sometimes clashing, sometimes thinning, never becoming one chord. Iris watched the sisters' breathing. Pella watched hands, eyes, current-time orientation. Talen watched Lian's language. Mara recorded lost work hours, care time, and the cost of keeping the road quiet around the family for two days after the session. Everything mattered. Not equally. But visibly.

The stabilization happened so quietly that no one noticed until Sefa spoke.

"The rain is farther away."

Pella leaned forward. "Gone?"

Sefa shook her head quickly. "No. Don't make it gone."

"I won't."

Lio touched the upside-down cup. "The heat is still there. But he is louder than it."

Halren laughed once, broken and offended. "I have always been loud."

Both daughters laughed and cried at the same time.

The field held for seven minutes.

Then Lian ended it before success could become appetite.

The outcome record took longer than the field:

`One household memory stabilized locally. Rain funeral and heat funeral remain unreconciled. Current-time Halren Moss strengthened as living anchor without invalidating daughters' grief memories. Cup retained as material witness with three meanings. No public release without family consent. No scale claim authorized. Follow-up care required in forty-eight hours.`

Sefa read it. Lio read it. Halren read it twice.

"Add that my tea remains bad," he said.

Iris looked at Pella.

Pella said, "Care-relevant continuity."

They added it.

Back at the tax office, the room received the result with a silence that wanted to become celebration and was not allowed.

Corin read the line `No scale claim authorized` aloud.

"Again," Vessa said.

He read it again.

The hostile Reckoner issued its assessment within minutes:

`LOCAL STABILIZATION CONFIRMED. IMPACT NONSCALABLE UNDER CURRENT STRUCTURE. MASS DRIFT CONTINUES.`

No one disputed it.

Venn added the response:

`Confirmed. Non-scalability is a safety feature and a strategic limitation. Do not erase either.`

That became the network's first success report.

It was tiny. One household. One cup. Two impossible funerals held beside one living father. No cure. No answer to the spreading collapse. No method a central authority could roll across the territories without destroying the conditions that made it work.

The reports kept coming.

Nareth Fold sent an update: Pell had asked for the bracket to be painted blue permanently. The fishing island requested written guidance but refused a cell. The northern salt road asked whether the sisters could become witnesses later, not now. Three Archive offices asked whether the success justified emergency expansion. The answer was no. Four Unwoven houses offered transport sponsorship. The answer was also no, pending custody terms. A Congregation splinter called the result proof that machines could be tamed by moral witness. Vessa's reply contained only the phrase `Do not turn a cup into doctrine.`

Corin copied the first success report into his field journal.

He did not write hope.

He wrote:

`The work that preserves cannot be scaled by force without becoming the thing it resists.`

Then he stared at the sentence until Lian leaned over and crossed out `the thing it resists`.

"Too grand," she said.

"Accurate?"

"Maybe. Still grand."

He rewrote:

`The work that preserves breaks when forced to move faster than consent.`

Lian nodded. "Better."

Outside, the city argued over ledgers, payments, predictions, protocols, and the first small memory. Inside, the witness net began training its second cell while the first slept under Pella's orders. The collapse continued. Families continued writing. Institutions continued trying to become necessary again. The hostile Reckoner continued predicting that local care would fail to meet mass conditions.

It was probably right.

It was not allowed to decide what replaced care.

At dusk, Iris returned from the salt road and placed a copied drawing beside Pell's hinge: a chipped cup, upside down, with rain on one side and sun on the other. Beneath it, Sefa and Lio had written together:

`He is alive in the room. We are allowed to remember grief without burying him.`

No one made it famous.

They pinned it inside the restricted training folder, where people learning the witness net would have to read the consent limits before seeing the drawing.

One memory had steadied.

The world had not.

For once, no one confused those facts.

The next morning, someone tried.

An Archive expansion office circulated a proposal titled `Witness Net Rapid Deployment Framework`, citing the salt road stabilization as proof of concept. The proposal had color bands, staffing projections, and a cheerful estimate that two hundred cells could be trained in six weeks under central coordination.

Lian read the first page, then placed it flat on the table as if it had become physically contaminated.

"No."

The aide who had brought it said, "It preserves the six-role model."

"It schedules consent in forty-minute intake windows."

"Only for planning."

"It assigns dissent holders from a central list."

"For reliability."

"It categorizes local authority by settlement type."

"For deployment speed."

Pella took the proposal and read the medical section. Her face went very calm. "It allows remote care lead review for low-risk households."

No one wanted Pella calm.

They convened an emergency rejection before the proposal could become reasonable in another office. Not because expansion was evil. Because this expansion had copied the shell and removed the inconvenience that made the first success ethical. The salt road cell had worked because Halren could make everyone wait, because the daughters controlled the account, because Talen's discomfort stayed local, because Lian adjusted only after plain consent, because the cup was not turned into a template before the family slept.

The rejection stated:

`The salt road outcome is not proof that the witness net can be centrally scaled. It is proof that a local cell can work when consent, dissent, care, cost, and technical limits remain close enough to interrupt each other. Rapid deployment that separates these conditions from local authority converts the result into extraction.`

Venn sent it to every office that had received the proposal.

The hostile Reckoner appended:

`REJECTION PRESERVES LOCAL INTEGRITY; MASS DRIFT RESPONSE REMAINS INADEQUATE.`

Corin surprised himself by answering, "Yes."

No one liked yes.

But yes kept the contradiction honest.

The contradiction became the final entry in the volume's working report:

`The witness net can stabilize some local memory drift without forced harmonization. The witness net cannot answer mass collapse at required speed. Central force can increase speed by destroying the consent conditions that make stabilization protective. Both facts must govern mass-collapse response planning.`

Corin read the entry aloud to the room.

No one cheered.

Then Iris, exhausted from two roads and three care queues, said, "Add that small success is still success."

Lian looked up. "That sounds sentimental."

"It is operational."

Pella supported the addition. "People continue care when small success is named. They abandon it when only insufficiency is named."

So the final line became:

`Small success is not enough; it is still part of enough if protected from becoming proof of more than it did.`

The sentence was awkward.

They kept it because clean language had been overperforming lately.

At the end of the day, Halren Moss sent a complaint.

Not a gratitude note. A complaint. The follow-up form asked whether the session had improved household functioning. He wrote that functioning was an office word and his daughters were not a cupboard. He suggested `Can you make tea and speak after?` as a better question.

Pella laughed for almost thirty seconds.

Then she changed the follow-up form.

The first small success had already corrected the network that recorded it.
